title: Write a SQL Query
description: Compose a correct, efficient SQL query from a question.
content:
Write a SQL query to answer: {{question}}

Schema:
{{schema}}

Requirements:
- Correct first: make sure joins and grouping answer the exact question.
- Avoid common traps: fan-out from one-to-many joins, NULL handling, double counting.
- Note which columns should be indexed for this to run well.
- Briefly explain what the query does so I can verify the logic.

title: Optimize a Slow Query
description: Diagnose and speed up a slow SQL query.
content:
This query is slow:

```
{{query}}
```

Diagnose it: what would the query plan likely show, and where's the cost (full scans, missing indexes, bad join order, sorting, functions on indexed columns)? Propose specific fixes — indexes, rewrites, or schema changes — and predict their effect. Tell me what to measure with EXPLAIN before and after.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis Plan
description: Plan a first pass over an unfamiliar dataset.
content:
I have a dataset and want to understand it: {{dataset}}

Give me an EDA plan: what to check first (shape, types, missingness, duplicates), the distributions and relationships worth plotting, the data-quality red flags to watch for, and the questions this data can and cannot answer. Prioritize the checks that most often reveal problems before they bite. Suggest concrete code if I tell you the tool.

title: Clean and Validate Data
description: Design a cleaning pipeline for messy data.
content:
Help me clean this data: {{data_description}}

Identify the cleaning steps in order: handle missing values (and justify drop vs. impute), fix types and formats, deduplicate, normalize categories, and catch outliers. For each step, state the rule and what could go wrong if applied blindly. Build in validation checks that fail loudly when the data violates expected invariants.

variables.name: data_description
variables.description: The shape and problems of the data

title: Interpret Results Honestly
description: Draw careful, caveated conclusions from an analysis.
content:
Here are my analysis results: {{results}}

Help me interpret them honestly. What can I legitimately conclude, and what would be over-reaching? Check for: confounders, correlation-vs-causation, sample-size/selection issues, multiple-comparison risk, and practical vs. statistical significance. Tell me the caveats I should state alongside any headline finding, and what follow-up would strengthen the conclusion.

title: Design a Metric
description: Define a metric that actually measures what you care about.
content:
I want to measure: {{goal}}

Help me design a good metric. Propose the precise definition (numerator, denominator, window, segment), explain what behavior it would drive, and stress-test it: how could it be gamed or give a misleading signal? Suggest a guardrail metric to pair with it. Prefer a metric that's slightly harder to compute but honest over an easy one that lies.

title: Build a Data Pipeline
description: Design an ETL/ELT pipeline that's reliable and observable.
content:
Design a data pipeline for: {{requirement}}

Cover: the stages (extract, transform, load), how you handle late/duplicate/malformed records, idempotency and re-runs, schema evolution, and how you'd know it broke (data-quality checks and alerting). Choose batch vs. streaming and justify it. Call out the step most likely to silently corrupt data and how you'd guard it.

title: Explain a Dataset Column
description: Reason about what a field means and how to use it.

variables.name: field
variables.description: The column/field name and any sample values
